The following conclusions were drawn from these measurements: the flange joint design appears to be satisfactory from an accumulated thermal stress standpoint after 43 cycles from room temperature to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and higher; the thermal distortion of the flanges is small in the operating range and is not of a nature to cause the flanges to separate at the seal; thermal cycling does not cause the gas leakage rate to increase above an acceptable level; the flange joint will not form a frozen plug in the pipe under no-flow conditions if the pipe heaters are kept on; the thermal and distortion properties of the INOR-8 flanges are superior to those of the Inconel flanges tested previously; and thermal cycling of one flange did not affect its ability to seal with a new flange.

A pair of INOR-8 freeze flanges for 5-in.-diam sched-40 pipe was installed in the freeze-flange-seal testing facility<sup>1</sup> for measurement of thermal distortion and gas leakage during high-temperature operation.
Following the successful completion of this series of tests, each of two flanges was mated with new INOR-8 flanges and placed into actual salt service.
This preliminary testing consisted of 10 cycles from a cold condition to a hot equilibrium condition.
The equilibrium temperatures were varied at $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ intervals from 800 to $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Heat was provided by 12 silicon carbide heating elements in the bore of the flange and by removable pipe-heaters along the exterior of the flange adaptor, beginning 2 l/4 in. from the flange faces.
At a bore temperature of $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the radial position of the freeze point was 7.1 in., which is believed to be satisfactorily far from the ring-gasket radius of 10.4 in.
The average decrease of the freeze-point radius was 11/16 in. for each $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ decrease in bore temperature.
The external thermal distortion of the 5-in.
INOR-8 flanges, both at temperature equilibrium and during temperature transients, was found to be much less than the thermal distortion of the 6-in.
Inconel flanges tested previously. $^{3}$ (The flanges differed only in material and bore diameter.) The INOR-8 flanges operated cooler than the Inconel flanges, as shown in Fig.
The distortions under equilibrium conditions are given in Table 3.l.

Starting with equilibrium bore temperatures of 1300 and $1400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , all power to the silicon carbide heating elements in the INOR-8 flanges was cut off to produce a transient simulating a salt dump.
When the bore temperature reached $850^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the maximum external distortion was 6 mils, indicating that little distortion is induced during temperature transients.
Upon opening the flange joint, however, the inner faces were found to be slightly convex; the axial displacement of the center of the bore was 14 mils.
Specifically, for the oval ring the cold leak rate steadily increased from $3.9 \times 10^{-8}$ to $1.4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}$ after five cycles.
There was initially no detectable leakage at high temperatures, but after five cycles the total leak rate was $1.5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}$ at $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ .
For the octagonal ring the initial cold leak rate was $8.5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}$ , and after five cycles, it was $4.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}$ .

At $1200^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ there was no initial detectable leakage, but after five cycles the leak rate had increased to $3.0 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~cm}^{3} / \mathrm{sec}$ at $1300^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ .

To simulate the expected condition of reactor components after heat treatment, the INOR-8 loop and container were heated to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and held for 12 hr with an oxygen-contaminated cover gas.
This is consistent with the expected increase of less than 1 ppm based on weight gain of INOR samples that had received similar treatment.
The loop was opened between operating periods at 800 to 1100 hr, but it was purged and held at a low bake-out temperature $(220^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ before refilling.
The first treatment (July 1962) of $74\mathrm{hr}$ with HF removed $42\mathrm{g}$ of oxygen, $^{12}$ equivalent to $175\mathrm{ppm}$ in the salt.
The second HF treatment lasted $38\mathrm{l}/2$ hr and removed only $9.5\mathrm{g}$ , or $40\mathrm{ppm}$ , of oxygen from the salt.

Although this quantity of oxygen was higher than expected, the actual rate of oxygen removal at the end of the treatment period was approximately $1/10$ of the maximum rate near the beginning of the period.
If there were oxide solids left in the bottom of the tank, it could mean that the dissolving rate was very low during the $400\mathrm{hr}$ between treatments.
The rate of corrosion of the container walls by the HF was apparently greater than during the treatment performed in the other tanks in November 1961.

There was a negligible increase in the salt chromium content during operation of the largely INOR-8 system, which is to be compared with the chromium content increase from 100 to 200 ppm during HF treatment of salt in the Inconel drain tank.

Considering the amount of tank surface exposed to the salt, the weight of salt treated, and the number of hours of treatment, the removal of chromium is equivalent to 0.00084 in. total or 0.01 mil per hour of HF treatment.

When the reactor was shut down, the power produced in capsules 24 and 36 dropped from 840 w to 14 w in 5 min, 7 w in 1 hr, 1.6 w in 100 hr, and 0.19 w in 95 days (calculated from the Way-Wigner equation).
Assuming that half of the energy released during a 95-day cooling period was absorbed in the fuel, a G value of only 0.035 would have been required to produce fluorine found in capsule 36.

The proposed simplification of the MSRE fuel mixture by the omission of $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ led to a more detailed study of the ternary system LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ , with a view toward prescribing suitable solvents to contain the 0.15 mole % UF $_4$ now needed for criticality.

The phase diagram of the solvent system did not change significantly, but new information on liquidus contours within the system established the peritectic point involving the solid phases LiF, 6LiF·BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ , and 2LiF·BeF $_2$ at a composition of LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ (67-29.5-3.5 mole %) and at $445^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

A minor surprise, in view of the rather unrestricted interchangeability of the quadrivalent cations $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ , $\mathrm{ThF_4}$ , and $\mathrm{UF_4}$ in the 10 to 15 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{MF_4}$ concentration range, was found in the fact that 7 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ gives a liquidus $25^{\circ}C$ higher than that of the 5-1-1 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF_4}-\mathrm{ThF_4}-\mathrm{UF_4}$ mixture when the remainder of the composition is 70 mole $\%$ LiF and 23 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ , as in the fuel that was nominally LiF- $\mathrm{BeF_2}-\mathrm{ZrF_4}-\mathrm{ThF_4}-\mathrm{UF_4}$ (70-23-5-1-1 mole $\%$ ).

Such a large change in liquidus temperature, or more specifically, in the freezing-point depression of LiF, for a change in composition of only 2 mole $\%$ is unusual in fluoride fuel mixtures and hence provides an interesting point for further analysis from the standpoint of the thermodynamics of solutions.
One of the specifications for the fuel was a melting point below $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ so that the fuel would not freeze before the coolant.

Since the 0.15 mole $\%$ $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_4$ in the fuel was expected to cause only a slight lowering of the liquidus, the fuel solvent was selected from the $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ contour in the LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ ternary system.

6.1, which depicts the $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ contour on a larger scale than that used previously, $^1$ the maximum LiF content occurs, in terms of the nearest whole numbers, near 67 mole $\%$ LiF, 29 mole $\%$ BeF $_2$ , and 4 mole $\%$ ZrF $_4$ .
The mixture $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ (66.85-29-4-0.15 mole %), with an estimated melting point of $445^{\circ}C$ , was therefore selected for particular study.

The obtuse corner in the $450^{\circ}C$ contour marks the change from LiF as a primary phase to 6LiF·BeF2·ZrF4, and for the fuel composition designated above the primary phase is expected to be 6LiF·BeF2-ZrF4.

Whether significant differences can be detected between liquids such as $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ZrF_4}$ (67-29-4 mole %) and $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ZrF_4}$ (67-30-3 mole %) is conjectural, but a richer $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ content may prove advantageous in
increasing the oxide tolerance of the fuel and in giving a primary phase that would be easier to melt than LiF (mp, $840^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) in case of accidental plugging by partial freezing.

Samples of compositions in the LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ system that contain more than $60\%$ BeF $_2$ and hence are too viscous to be of present interest as fuels were quenched from temperatures above the liquidus.

An assumed sequence of equilibrium fractionations in the freezing of the five-component MSRE fuel mixture $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ZrF_4 - ThF_4 - UF_4}$ (70-23-5-1-1 mole %) was studied by quenching tests to provide information regarding (1) the fraction of $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ remaining in the liquid state at the onset of crystallization of phases containing $\mathrm{UF_4}$ and whether this $\mathrm{ZrF_4}$ concentration was adequate for protection against precipitation of $\mathrm{UO_2}$ , (2) the concentration of uranium in the crystalline equilibrium phases within which it is contained during freezing and the relative position of these phases in the sequence of crystallization reactions, and (3) the approximate concentration of $\mathrm{BeF_2}$ in the liquid remaining at temperatures just above the solidus.
As noted previously,3 the limit of protection can be exceeded.
The apparent solidus temperature for the five-component mixture is $429^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Comparison with solidus values in the limiting $\mathsf{LiF - BeF_2 - }$ $\mathsf{MF}_4$ systems implies that the composition of the five-component liquid at the solidus consists of no more than 40 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{BeF_2}$ .
The last solid phase observed on freezing the fuel mixture is 7LiF-6(U,Th) $\mathsf{F_4}$ , which contains 13.3 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{UF_4}$ .
The proportion of $\mathsf{UF_4}$ was determined by two independent methods: (1) by calculation of the material balance and (2) from measurements of the refractive indices of the solid solution.
Three nonhygroscopic solid phases were found in melts cooled under equilibrium conditions: 6LiF $\cdot$ BeF $_2$ $\cdot$ ZrF $_4$ , 2LiF $\cdot$ BeF $_2$ , and 7LiF $\cdot$ 6(U,Th) $\mathsf{F_4}$ .

It is to be noted that specimens obtained from a variety of experiments and experimental engineering tests, in which equilibrium cooling did not occur, often contain 5LiF $\cdot$ ZrF $_4$ , 2LiF $\cdot$ ZrF $_4$ , 2LiF $\cdot$ BeF $_2$ , and 7LiF $\cdot$ 6(U,Th) $\mathsf{F_4}$ , rather than the equilibrium solids.
The $\mathrm{Cs}^+$ and $\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}$ ion sites were determined, and the $\mathbb{F}^-$ ions were shown to be in a disordered arrangement.
The face-centered cubic unit cell has $a = 9.70\mathrm{A}$ .
The rhombohedral unit cell has a = 5.73 A and $\alpha = 56^{\circ}40'$ , and it contains two $\mathrm{InF}_3$ formula weights.
The crystal density is $4.64~\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ .
The equilibrium inversion temperature for the pure $\mathrm{NaYF_4}$ crystals inverting from the high-temperature fluorite form to the low-temperature hexagonal form is $691^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

During continuing investigations of the phase relationships in the system NaF- $\mathrm{YF_3}$ , it was discovered that at nearly saturating concentrations of $\mathrm{YF_3}$ in the fluorite cubic solid solution, $\mathrm{NaYF_4 - YF_3}$ , solid solutions consisting of approximately 65 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{YF_3}$ appear as equilibrium phases to temperatures below $475^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Hence the cubic form of $\mathrm{NaYF_4}$ is stabilized against inversion to the low-temperature form to the extent of approximately $15^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for each mole percent of dissolved $\mathrm{YF_3}$ .

In one set of experiments, gas evolution from graphite blocks placed in a circulating stream of helium at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is being studied.

Initially the reactor core of the MSRE will be heated to about $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at a maximum rate of approximately $30^{\circ}\mathrm{C/hr}$ while preheated helium is circulated through the core at about 75 liters/sec.

Accordingly, pieces of graphite (6 to 12 in. in length) were canned in a manner that provided the same cross-sectional configuration for flow as an MSRE moderator element, and gas entry and exit ports were provided at either end.

Based on a uniform gas velocity through the MSRE reactor core, a flow rate of about 2.5 liters/min through the experimental loop was assumed to approximate gas purge conditions around each moderator element.

The predominant characteristics of the gas evolution were the removal of physically absorbed moisture at temperatures below $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the removal of chemisorbed moisture over the temperature interval 200 to $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
